# Availability of publications {#sec-OApubl}


## Open publications

There are several routes for making publications openly available, each with different cost structures, embargo conditions, and implications for funder compliance. For a concise overview of Open Access (OA) publishing, see @suber2012openaccess.

- **Gold OA**: Publishing in a fully open-access journal where all content is immediately and permanently free to read without restrictions. Authors usually pay an Article Processing Charge (APC), through this varies widely by journal and discipline.
- **Hybrid OA**: A subscription journal that allows individual articles to be made open access for a fee. Some funder mandates (e.g., [Plan S](https://www.coalition-s.org/), [UKRI](https://www.ukri.org/publications/ukri-open-access-policy/), [NIH](https://publicaccess.nih.gov/)) increasingly require immediate public access to accepted manuscripts, with no embargo under updated policies, which limits how straightforward hybrid options are. Under plan S in particular, hybrid journals are generally only compliant when covered by transformative agreements or formal transition pathways.
- **Diamond / Platinum OA**: Journals that are fully open access with no charges for authors or readers. These are typically supported by universities, societies, or library consortia. Several music journals follow this model (see below). For a broader overview, see @bosman2021oa.
- **Green OA (self-archiving)**: Depositing the accepted manuscript in a repository such as [Zenodo](https://zenodo.org/), [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/) (see below), or an institutional repository. Policies differ by publisher, especially around embargo length and which versions can be shared: [Jisc - Open policy finder](https://openpolicyfinder.jisc.ac.uk/oa-compliance) can be used for checking journal-specific policies. In the EU, [Horizon Europe](https://research-and-innovation.ec.europa.eu/open-science_en) encourages this route and expects immediate open access to accepted manuscrits via repositories.
- **APC costs and waivers**: APCs range from a few hundred to several thousand USD or EUR. Some publishers offer waivers or discounts for researchers without funding or from low-income countries, so it is worth checking before assuming APC-based OA is not feasible.

## OA publishing in numbers

Open access publishing has grown substantially over the past decade, though the landscape remains uneven. Between 2014 and 2024, the share of global scholarly articles made available 
via gold OA rose from 14% to 40%, while the subscription-only share fell from 70% to 54% [@stm2024dashboard]. This shift has been driven in part by funder mandates, but also by the 
rapid growth of APC-funded publishing, which carries its own equity concerns: annual global APC spending across six major publishers alone almost tripled from \$910 million in 2019 to \$2.5 billion in 2023 [@haustein2024estimating].

These figures describe the overall scholarly publishing market. Music research sits within this landscape but has its own characteristics: a mix of humanities, social science, and engineering publication norms; a strong conference culture in MIR; and a notable cluster of diamond OA journals (TISMIR, EMR) that charge neither authors nor readers. The table below summarises the main OA routes relevant to music researchers.


| Route | Cost to author | Cost to reader | Embargo | Funder compliance (Plan S / UKRI) | Music examples |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gold OA | APC required (avg ~$1,626; up to $9,900) | Free | None | Generally compliant (if CC licensing meets policy) | *Music & Science*, *Frontiers in Psychology* |
| Diamond OA | No APC | Free | None |     Generally compliant | *TISMIR*, *Empirical Musicology Review* |
| Hybrid OA | APC required | Free (OA articles only) | None |     Conditionally compliant | *Music Perception*, *JNMR* |
| Green OA | No APC | Free via repository | Often 6–24 months |     Conditionally compliant (policy dependent) | Most musicology journals; arXiv (preprints for MIR and ML-adjacent work) |
| Subscription only | No charge | Paywalled | — |     Not compliant (OA policies) | Many humanities and society journals |

: APC average from @morrison2022change. Funder compliance reflects Plan S principles as of 2024. {.small}

## Preprints

Preprints, i.e., versions of manuscripts shared publicly before or alongside formal peer review, are now a widely used mechanism for rapid and open dissemination. For practical guidance on posting and managing preprints across the publication pipeline, see @moshontz2021guide.

- **Where to post** (varies by subdiscipline):
  - [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/) (categories `cs.SD` and `eess.AS`) for Music Information Retrieval (MIR), music technology, and Machine Leanring(ML)-adjacent work  
  - [PsyArXiv](https://psyarxiv.com/) for music psychology  
  - [OSF Preprints](https://osf.io/preprints/) for interdisciplinary work  
  - [Zenodo](https://zenodo.org/) for general-purpose dissemination, including datasets and code alongside papers  
  - [SSRN](https://www.ssrn.com/) for social science and related interdisciplinary work  
  - [HAL](https://hal.science/) for computer science, engineering, humanities, and social sciences


- **Subdiscipline norms vary widely**: Preprinting is well established in MIR and ML-adjacent music research, where posting on arXiv prior to or alongside ISMIR, ICASSP, or NeurIPS submissions is standard practice. Uptake is still uneven in music psychology and remains limited in musicology and music education, although adoption is gradually increasing.

- **Versioning**: When a preprint is later published, the two versions should be explicitly linked (for example via DOI cross-references). Some repositories, such as PsyArXiv, support metadata linking that helps citation systems merge records across versions.

- **Journal prior-posting policies**: Most journals now allow preprints, but conditions still vary regarding timing, licensing, and accepted manuscript versions. It is advisable to verify specific policies via [Jisc - Open policy finder](https://openpolicyfinder.jisc.ac.uk/) before submission.

- **Citation advantage of preprints**: Several large-scale studies report that papers with an associated preprint tend to receive earlier and often higher citation counts compared to those without preprints, likely due to earlier visibility and dissemination (e.g., @serghiou2018altmetric, @fraser2020relationship).

- **Academic sharing platforms**: Services such as [ResearchGate](https://www.researchgate.net/) and [Academia.edu](https://www.academia.edu/) are often used to upload and circulate papers, including preprints. However, they are not formal preprint repositories and do not provide consistent long-term preservation or clear policy compliance guarantees. They function more as academic networking and sharing spaces than as archival infrastructure.

- **Secondary interpretation tools**: Services such as arXivExplained and similar paper summarisation tools sit one step removed from publication. They help make preprints easier to read and explore, but they are not repositories and do not host canonical versions of research outputs. They are interpretation layers rather than publication infrastructure.


## Journal and conference landscape by subdiscipline

The following list of representative journals and major venues is not exhaustive. In addition, OA status and APC policies can change, so verify on the journal's website before submitting.

**Music psychology**

- [*Music Perception*](https://online.ucpress.edu/mp) — subscription (UC Press), hybrid OA option available
- [*Music & Science*](https://journals.sagepub.com/home/mns) — fully OA (SAGE), APC applies (waivers available)
- [*Psychomusicology: Music, Mind, and Brain*](https://www.apa.org/pubs/journals/pmu/) — subscription (APA), hybrid OA option
- [*Empirical Musicology Review*](https://emusicology.org/) — fully OA, no APC; diamond model, published by The Ohio State University Libraries
- [*Frontiers in Psychology* (Auditory Cognitive Neuroscience section)](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/psychology) — fully OA, APC applies; used widely for music perception and cognition work

**Computational musicology / MIR**

- [*Transactions of the International Society for Music Information Retrieval (TISMIR)*](https://transactions.ismir.net/) — fully OA, no APC; diamond model and the flagship journal of the MIR community; also actively encourages open data and code archiving alongside submissions
- [*Journal of New Music Research*](https://www.tandfonline.com/journals/nnmr20) — subscription (Taylor & Francis), hybrid OA option
- [*Computer Music Journal*](https://direct.mit.edu/comj) — subscription (MIT Press), hybrid OA option
- [*ACM Transactions on Multimedia Computing, Communications, and Applications (TOMM)*](https://dl.acm.org/journal/tomm) — subscription with OA option; covers music retrieval and multimedia processing
- [*IEEE/ACM Transactions on Audio, Speech, and Language Processing (TASLP)*](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/xpl/RecentIssue.jsp?punumber=6570655) — subscription with OA option; a principal journal for audio/music ML methods

**Musicology**

Musicology journals are often published by learned societies (American Musicological Society, Royal Musical Association, Society for Music Analysis) or major humanities presses (Oxford, Cambridge, Chicago). Fully OA options are limited; Green OA via author self-archiving is frequently the most realistic route. OA monograph publishing is underdeveloped in this space, which matters because the monograph remains a primary output form in musicology.

**Music education**

Music education journals span a range of publishers and have uneven OA uptake. The [*Journal of Research in Music Education*](https://journals.sagepub.com/home/jrm) and [*Music Education Research*](https://www.tandfonline.com/journals/cmue20) both offer hybrid OA options.

**Cross-disciplinary / psychology-adjacent**

- [*Collabra: Psychology*](https://online.ucpress.edu/collabra) — fully OA, APC applies but with a liberal waiver policy; suitable for music psychology work following open science practices

**AI and music technology conferences**

Much of the most impactful music AI research is first published at conferences, not journals. OA status varies by venue:

- [ISMIR](https://ismir.net/) — fully OA proceedings; the primary venue for MIR and computational music research
- [ICASSP](https://signalprocessingsociety.org/conferences-events/icassp) (IEEE) — IEEE Xplore, subscription access; widely used for audio and music signal processing
- [NeurIPS](https://nips.cc/), [ICML](https://icml.cc/), [ICLR](https://iclr.cc/) — open proceedings; music-adjacent work on generative models, audio representations, and music understanding appears here with increasing frequency
- [AAAI](https://aaai.org/conference/aaai/) — open proceedings on AAAI Digital Library; has received music-related submissions across generation, recommendation, and analysis
- [NIME](https://www.nime.org/) (New Interfaces for Musical Expression) — open proceedings; relevant for music technology and creative practice
- [SMC](https://smcnetwork.org/) (Sound and Music Computing) — open proceedings; bridges signal processing, AI, and musicology

A note on conference vs. journal culture: in MIR and music AI, conference papers at ISMIR, ICASSP, NeurIPS, and related venues carry significant weight and are treated as primary research contributions, in contrast to humanities norms where journal articles and monographs are expected.



## Authorship / Attribution of credit / ORCIDs

Authorship conventions and credit attribution vary across music research subdisciplines and are a recurring source of ambiguity, particularly in large or interdisciplinary collaborations. For a foundational discussion of author sequence and contribution in multiauthored publications, see @tscharntke2007author.

- **Authorship criteria**: Most publishers follow ICMJE-style guidelines requiring that authors have contributed substantially to conception, execution, or interpretation; drafted or critically revised the work; approved the final version; and agreed to be accountable for it. Being named in acknowledgements is not authorship.
- **CRediT (Contributor Roles Taxonomy)**: The CRediT taxonomy provides 14 standardised roles (Conceptualization, Data Curation, Formal Analysis, Funding Acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project Administration, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – Original Draft, Writing – Review & Editing) for explicitly attributing what each contributor did [@brand2015credit]. It is now supported by all major publishers and is an ANSI/NISO standard. CRediT does not determine who qualifies as an author; it makes contributions transparent within the author list and beyond it.
- **ORCIDs**: [ORCID](https://orcid.org/) (Open Researcher and Contributor ID) is a persistent, unique digital identifier for researchers, free to register. It disambiguates authors across name changes, transliteration variants, or common surnames, and links research outputs, affiliations, and grants to a single record. Most major journals and funding agencies now require or strongly encourage ORCID registration at submission. Registering and maintaining an ORCID record is particularly important for early-career researchers whose publication record is still accumulating, and for researchers with names that are common or that vary across publications.
- **Disciplinary variation**: Authorship norms differ sharply across subdisciplines. In MIR and music technology, large multi-author collaborative papers (e.g., shared datasets, benchmarks, evaluation campaigns) are increasingly common and benefit most from CRediT transparency. In musicology, single- and dual-author papers remain the norm, and the concept of contributorship is less familiar. Music psychology sits closer to psychology norms, where author contribution statements are increasingly standard.
- **AI authorship**: There is emerging consensus that generative AI tools (LLMs, etc.) cannot be listed as authors, as they cannot take responsibility for the work. Their use should instead be disclosed in a methods or acknowledgements statement. Several major publishers (Elsevier, Springer Nature, PLOS) have adopted explicit policies to this effect.
